kept.text:
Cleveland‑Cliffs Announces Multi-Year Partnership with Palantir to Deploy AI Platform

$CLF today announced it has entered a strategic partnership with $PLTR to deploy advanced AI‑driven solutions across its footprint. The recently executed three‑year agreement puts Palantir’s best-in-class AI technology at the center of Cliffs’ key internal processes in operations and commercial.

The partnership represents a significant step forward in Cleveland‑Cliffs’ ongoing effort to modernize its systems across its manufacturing footprint. By leveraging Palantir’s enterprise AI platform, Cliffs is embedding artificial intelligence directly into production planning, order entry, and operational workflows to better integrate data, anticipate constraints, and coordinate activities across its facilities in real time. 

As AI becomes an increasingly important driver of competitiveness across all industrial businesses, the partnership with Palantir reflects Cleveland‑Cliffs’ broader focus on deploying advanced technology to drive productivity, improve performance, and position the company ahead of the curve when compared to others in the United States and abroad.

Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs’ Chairman, President, and CEO said: “After completing our pilot work with Palantir, it became clear they were the platform of choice to take our business into the future. What we have seen so far has been nothing short of a gamechanger. Integrated steelmaking is extraordinarily complex and Palantir’s platform allows us to solve problems in ways humans simply cannot. In addition, our cultural and policy alignment with Palantir make this partnership a very natural and durable fit for Cliffs.”

profile_bio.description: All things Palantir USG. Saving The Shire 1 post at a time.

kept.text:
Forbes with an interesting piece about building AI that is offline (centered around $PLTR and Anduril)

Palantir And Anduril Build Offline AI And That’s No Edge Case

Palantir and others have more AI and Agents on the Edge. Why? Over 80% of global trade by volume moves by sea, according to the United Nations. Oil rigs operate hundreds of miles offshore. Mining operations run in deserts with no reliable connectivity. Military assets make decisions in contested environments where a round trip to the cloud is measured in seconds that nobody has.

Yet the vast majority of enterprise AI being built today assumes a stable internet connection, abundant power, and proximity to a data center. For the industries that move the global economy and protect national security, that assumption is a liability.

A new class of companies is building AI that works where the infrastructure does not. And the biggest names in defense technology are leading the charge.

Palantir and Saildrone Take Maritime AI Autonomous
Saildrone and Palantir Technologies recently announced a strategic partnership to advance AI powered maritime intelligence by integrating Palantir's AI infrastructure with Saildrone's autonomous surface vehicles. The collaboration targets maritime security, surveillance, and operational scalability, delivering edge-deployed intelligence for defense and domain awareness where crewed assets cannot always go.

The partnership represents a significant shift in how military and civilian maritime operators think about persistent ocean surveillance. Rather than depending on crewed vessels or satellite passes with hours-long latency, the Saildrone and Palantir integration places decision-grade intelligence directly on the water, continuously, in locations that would be impractical or dangerous for human crews.

The AI Questions Leaders Should Be Asking
These companies operate in different industries with different customers, and they are all solving the same structural problem: AI that depends on the cloud fails in the environments where decisions carry the highest stakes.

For business leaders deploying AI in field operations, distributed facilities, logistics networks, or any environment where connectivity is inconsistent, three questions should precede any enterprise AI contract. How close is the intelligence to the actual decision point? Does it function when connectivity, power, or infrastructure are limited? And can your AI perform when the infrastructure underneath it disappears?

The companies generating the greatest impact in physical AI right now are the ones that answered yes to all three before they wrote a single line of code. As AI moves from the data center into the real world, the winners will be defined by where their intelligence works and that’s no edge case. Palantir, Pollentia, and more are already focused on the edge!

kept.text:
$PLTR X $CLF Cleveland‑Cliffs Announces Multi-Year Partnership with Palantir to Deploy AI Platform
Apr 28, 2026
CLEVELAND--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (NYSE: CLF) today announced it has entered a strategic partnership with Palantir Technologies to deploy advanced AI‑driven solutions across its footprint. The recently executed three‑year agreement puts Palantir’s best-in-class AI technology at the center of Cliffs’ key internal processes in operations and commercial.

The partnership represents a significant step forward in Cleveland‑Cliffs’ ongoing effort to modernize its systems across its manufacturing footprint. By leveraging Palantir’s enterprise AI platform, Cliffs is embedding artificial intelligence directly into production planning, order entry, and operational workflows to better integrate data, anticipate constraints, and coordinate activities across its facilities in real time. As AI becomes an increasingly important driver of competitiveness across all industrial businesses, the partnership with Palantir reflects Cleveland‑Cliffs’ broader focus on deploying advanced technology to drive productivity, improve performance, and position the company ahead of the curve when compared to others in the United States and abroad.

Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs’ Chairman, President, and CEO said: “After completing our pilot work with Palantir, it became clear they were the platform of choice to take our business into the future. What we have seen so far has been nothing short of a gamechanger. Integrated steelmaking is extraordinarily complex and Palantir’s platform allows us to solve problems in ways humans simply cannot. In addition, our cultural and policy alignment with Palantir make this partnership a very natural and durable fit for Cliffs.”

About Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

Cleveland-Cliffs is a leading North America-based steel producer with focus on value-added sheet products, particularly for the automotive industry. The Company is vertically integrated from the mining of iron ore, production of pellets and direct reduced iron, and processing of ferrous scrap through primary steelmaking and downstream finishing, stamping, tooling, and tubing. Headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio, Cleveland-Cliffs employs approximately 25,000 people across its operations in the United States and Canada.
🇺🇸🇨🇦🦾🔮🔥

We get into the development of their flagship Leonidas system, designed to neutralize drone swarms, and how this technology is changing modern combat. Andy shares his background as a U.S. Navy nuclear engineer serving aboard an aircraft carrier before transitioning into leadership roles at Raytheon and into the startup world. kept.text:
パラ友各位

$PLTR が新たな特許を取得しました。
お時間ある方はご一読下さい🐕
$PLTR #palantir #パランティア #米国株

US 12,613,920 B2（Higgins et al.）

▷発明名：Enhanced processing of time series data via parallelization of instructions

▷出願人：Palantir Technologies Inc.

▷要点
・時系列データに対するクエリを受け取る
・処理内容をquery tree（処理ツリー）として分解
・複数ノードへ処理を並列分散
・結果を統合して高速に返す

▷技術的本質
・時系列データを逐次処理するのではなく、命令を並列化して高速処理する仕組み
・大量ログ・センサーデータ分析のスケーラビリティ向上に直結

▷まとめ
この特許は、 $PLTR が単に「データを保存・分析する」だけでなく、巨大な時系列データをリアルタイム級で処理する基盤性能そのものを強化していることを示しています。
つまり、「大量データをどう高速に処理するか」のレイヤーを担っています。
さらに言うと、「データOS」の計算基盤そのものまで最適化しているということです。

▷用語簡易説明
☑︎Time series data
時間順に並んだデータ（センサーログ、株価、監視データなど）

☑︎Parallelization
処理を分割して同時並行で実行すること

☑︎Query tree
処理内容を木構造に分解したもの

☑︎Node
処理単位や計算ノード

☑︎Scalability
データ量増加に耐えられる拡張性

kept.text:
パラ友各位

@PalantirTech × @CLE_CLF 🔥

新たなパートナーシップの締結です。
着々と契約増やしてる。
株価はついてくる。

お時間ある方はご一読下さい🐕
※原文はリプ欄へ
※@CLE_CLF についてもリプ欄へ
$PLTR #palantir #パランティア #米国株

Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.、AIプラットフォーム導入に向けてPalantir Technologiesとの複数年パートナーシップを発表

米国オハイオ州クリーブランド–(BUSINESS WIRE)– Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.（NYSE: CLF）は本日、同社の事業全体において先進的なAI駆動型ソリューションを導入するため、Palantir Technologiesと戦略的パートナーシップ契約を締結したと発表しました。今回締結された3年間の契約により、Palantirの業界最高水準のAI技術が、Cliffsのオペレーションおよび商業部門における主要な社内プロセスの中核に据えられます。

この提携は、Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.が進めている製造拠点全体のシステム近代化に向けた取り組みにおいて、大きな前進を意味します。Cliffsは、Palantirのエンタープライズ向けAIプラットフォームを活用することで、生産計画、受注入力、業務ワークフローにAIを直接組み込み、データ統合を強化するとともに、制約要因を事前に予測し、各拠点の活動をリアルタイムで連携・調整できるようにします。

AIがあらゆる産業分野において競争力を左右する重要な要素となる中で、今回のPalantir Technologiesとの提携は、生産性向上、パフォーマンス改善、そして米国内外の競合他社に先駆けたポジション確立を目指し、先進技術を積極的に導入していくというCliffsの広範な方針を反映したものです。

Cliffsの会長兼社長兼CEOであるLourenco Goncalvesは次のように述べています。

「Palantir Technologiesとのパイロットプロジェクトを完了した後、同社こそが私たちの事業を未来へ導くために最適なプラットフォームであることが明確になりました。これまで私たちが目にしてきた成果は、まさにゲームチェンジャーと言えるものです。統合型製鉄事業は極めて複雑ですが、Palantirのプラットフォームによって、人間だけでは到底解決できない方法で問題を解決できるようになります。さらに、私たちとPalantirは企業文化や方針面でも一致しており、このパートナーシップはCliffsにとって非常に自然で、長期的に持続可能なものになると考えています。」

kept.text:
$PLTR Partner @GardaWorldC24 Crisis24 Survey Reveals Boardroom Blindspot: Majority of Leaders Claim to be Prepared for the Next Crisis. Their Own Answers Suggest Otherwise. 👀⬇️
New Crisis24 survey reveals a "preparedness paradox" among U.S. senior executives, with widespread confidence masking persistent blind spots in crisis detection and response

Apr 28, 2026
ANNAPOLIS, Md., April 28, 2026 /CNW/ - A new survey of 303 senior U.S. business leaders, conducted by The Harris Poll on behalf of Crisis24, a global, AI-enhanced provider in integrated risk management, intelligence-led security and medical operations, personal protection, medical concierge and crisis consulting, has found a striking disconnect between how prepared executives believe they are for major disruptions and what the data actually shows about their track record.

Every respondent described their organization's approach to crisis planning as proactive. Every leader said they were confident in their ability to spot emerging risks ahead of competitors. Yet when pressed on specifics, a different picture emerged: 93% admitted their company has missed warning signs of crises or disruptions, with one in four saying it happens frequently or all the time. Nearly half (48%) agreed that their leadership team is frequently caught off guard by market shifts and external pressures.

The findings point to what Crisis24 is calling a "preparedness paradox": a gap between boardroom confidence and operational reality that leaves companies exposed, often without realizing it.

"This data should be a wake-up call," said Sid Kosaraju, President at Crisis24. "Leaders genuinely believe they are ready for crises and disruptions, but when nearly all of them also admit to missing warning signs, that confidence starts to look like a vulnerability in itself. The organizations that will come through the next crisis in the strongest position are the ones willing to challenge their own assumptions about how ready they really are. Putting in place the right structures, processes and tools also enables organizations to better seize opportunities during volatile times."

Blind spots carry a measurable cost

The survey quantified the financial impact of missed signals. Every respondent reported that their business had suffered a financial impact from a recent disruption, with more than a quarter (28%) putting the cost at $25 million or more from a single event. The median impact was $2 million.

Senior leaders also estimated that close to half (46%) of their leadership team's time is spent reacting to immediate crises rather than preparing for future ones. And when asked whether lost market share over the past decade could have been prevented with earlier access to relevant intelligence, 60% said more than a quarter of those losses were avoidable.

Even as companies invest more in planning and intelligence, the preparedness paradox shows confidence is masking risk. Connect with Crisis24 to identify blind spots before they escalate.

Geopolitical instability was already a top concern before the Iran conflict

The survey was fielded in Q1 2026, before the outbreak of the US/Israel-Iran conflict. Even then, the data showed that geopolitical risk was weighing heavily on the C-suite.

Nearly four in five respondents (79%) said that recent geopolitical events had forced their company to rethink its crisis management strategy. Forty-five percent said they felt underprepared for geopolitical instability specifically. And more than a quarter (26%) of leadership teams described their anxiety about global instability's impact on their business as high or severe, with 81% agreeing that this anxiety has a direct impact on strategic decision-making in their organization.

"These findings were concerning enough earlier in the year," said Sid Kosaraju, President, Crisis24. "Since the outbreak of the conflict in the Middle East, the global risk environment has only become more complex. For companies operating internationally, or with supply chains that touch volatile regions, the ability to see disruptions coming is no longer a nice-to-have. It's a strategic necessity."

Too much data, not enough clarity

The survey identified a clear tension between the volume of information organizations collect and their ability to act on it. Two-thirds (67%) of leaders agreed that their organization has access to large amounts of data but struggles to convert it into prioritized, actionable insights. More than half (56%) pointed to a disconnect between the data they collect and their ability to use it for fast strategic decisions during crises.

When asked what prevents their company from detecting crises earlier, the top barriers were information overload (46%), too much noise in the data (43%), and difficulty establishing data credibility or relevance (42%). In addition, 68% cited some form of inability to forecast business disruptions as a barrier, including lack of suitable external solutions, in-house capabilities, and dedicated resources.

Leaders are looking to AI to close the gap

Despite the challenges, the survey found strong appetite for technology-driven solutions. Eighty-five percent of respondents identified increased use of AI as a top growth opportunity for their company in 2026, well ahead of new product launches (64%), market expansion (56%) and digital transformation (57%). When asked what would most strengthen their resilience over the next three years, greater use of AI again came first (63%), followed by investment in new technology and analytics (50%) and better forecasting and predictive information (49%).

The demand for early warning capability was near-universal. Eighty-two percent said that having advance notice of the next major crisis affecting their business would deliver game-changing or significant strategic value. The majority (57%) said three or six months of lead time would be the most valuable window.

These findings align closely with the capabilities of the Crisis24 AiiA Powered by Palantir platform, which launched earlier this year. Crisis24 AiiA is an anticipatory intelligence tool designed to help leadership teams identify emerging threats and opportunities before they reach the boardroom as surprises. It sits alongside Crisis24's established network of intelligence analysts, global operations centers and on-the-ground crisis response capabilities.

"What this survey tells us is that leaders aren't short on data or on good intentions," said Ansel Stein, VP Product Evangelism, Crisis24 AiiA. "What they need is a way to cut through the noise and get to the signals that actually matter, with enough lead time to act. That's exactly the problem AiiA was built to solve."

The risks leaders want to see coming

When asked which potential crises they feel most underprepared for, senior leaders pointed to cybersecurity threats (48%), AI-driven misinformation (46%), geopolitical instability (45%) and financial market instability (42%).

The signals they most want to monitor tell a similar story: cyber and digital threats (53%), supply chain vulnerabilities (37%) and financial market movement impacts (35%) topped the list...

kept.text:
Executive Vice President at Palantir Technologies (@PalantirTech) Louis Mosley (@louismosley) shares the exact extent and chronological relationship of their work with the NHS: 

"They [NHS] were struggling to identify the number of COVID patients, how fast the pandemic was spreading,  how many hospital beds were available".

"We were brought in based on the experience that we had and the credibility that we built on the military side to then come and help tackle the pandemic".

"Off the back of that they they commissioned us for two years to to continue that work we've been doing during COVID". 

"What we also started doing in parallel, which is what led to the federated data platform, was to begin working with some of the individual trusts and the challenge that they had, what they called the elective backlog". 

"We are helping people get their operations faster".

kept.text:
BREAKING $PLTR signed 3-yr w/ $CLF 🚀

Cleveland‑Cliffs Announces Multi-Year Partnership with @PalantirTech  to Deploy AI Platform

CLEVELAND--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (NYSE: CLF) today announced it has entered a strategic partnership with Palantir Technologies to deploy advanced AI‑driven solutions across its footprint. The recently executed three‑year agreement puts Palantir’s best-in-class AI technology at the center of Cliffs’ key internal processes in operations and commercial.

The partnership represents a significant step forward in Cleveland‑Cliffs’ ongoing effort to modernize its systems across its manufacturing footprint. By leveraging Palantir’s enterprise AI platform, Cliffs is embedding artificial intelligence directly into production planning, order entry, and operational workflows to better integrate data, anticipate constraints, and coordinate activities across its facilities in real time. As AI becomes an increasingly important driver of competitiveness across all industrial businesses, the partnership with Palantir reflects Cleveland‑Cliffs’ broader focus on deploying advanced technology to drive productivity, improve performance, and position the company ahead of the curve when compared to others in the United States and abroad.

Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs’ Chairman, President, and CEO said: “After completing our pilot work with Palantir, it became clear they were the platform of choice to take our business into the future. What we have seen so far has been nothing short of a gamechanger. Integrated steelmaking is extraordinarily complex and Palantir’s platform allows us to solve problems in ways humans simply cannot. In addition, our cultural and policy alignment with Palantir make this partnership a very natural and durable fit for Cliffs.”

quoted_tweet.text:
$PLTR "Secret Sauce" is the 🐐
Continuous Margin Expansion Loop- The Hack🧵

Palantir achieved its "insane" margin expansion (adjusted operating margins climbing from low/negative pre-2023 levels to 39% in FY2024, 50% in FY2025, and a record 57% in Q4 2025, with GAAP operating margins hitting 41% in Q4 2025) through a deliberate, multi-year shift from a high-touch, services-heavy custom-integration model to a highly scalable, product-led software platform business.
Palantir culture tackles the most complex problem as a platform, engineers are encouraged to build softwares that are 5-10 years ahead. This was 2 decades in the seed planting to get us to where we are. 

1. The "secret sauce"

Palantir has always operated with a multi-year (or decade-long) time horizon, rather than chasing quarterly features or easy wins. Dr. Karp and FDE are explicitly encouraged to tackle the hardest, most complex problems that others avoid   things like integrating messy, real-world enterprise/government data at massive scale while maintaining governance, auditability, and "magical" frontline outcomes

When institutions realized Palantir software is Free, where Palantir enables massive saving for enterprises across the world and the US govt, Valuation earns a premium. To be more precise, highest margin in the SaaS or any company to be frank. We are talking about Operating Margin, not even try to debate Gross Margin here. 

This "build far ahead" approach meant years of negative/low margins and heavy spending on elite talent + forward-deployed engineers. But it created a moat: Once the Ontology is in place for a customer, additional use cases, agents, and expansions have near-zero marginal cost. That's the core of the recent margin ramp (software-like scalability on top of complex problems).

2.  The 2023 Inflection: AIP Launch + Bootcamp Model (Product-Led Acceleration)

~AIP launched in 2023 was the game-changer. It layers generative AI/LLMs directly onto the Ontology + Gotham/Foundry. This lets customers rapidly build/deploy AI "agents" and workflows that are grounded in real business rules (no hallucinations), with full governance, provenance, and auditability.

~AIP Bootcamps (rolled out alongside AIP): These are 5-day intensive, hands-on sessions where Palantir engineers work with the customer's team on the customer's own data to build live, production-ready AI use cases/workflows. 

~Replaces months-long custom pilots with quick, low/no-cost demos from original sale phase
Acquire - Expand - Scale. 
This Dramatically shortens sales cycles and conversion. Shifts to product-led growth, customers see immediate value and expand organically (one energy customer scaled from ~$4M to >$20M ACV in a year via 4 → 280+ use cases).
Basically AIP - Scale now!

~Ontology as the reusable moat: Once the semantic layer is built for the first use case(s), every additional app, agent, or workflow has near-zero marginal cost. This drives insane net dollar retention (139% in Q4 2025) and within-account expansion without proportional new spending. 

3. "Secret Sauce" results in Operating Leverage that drove this continous margin expansion that is unheard of

High gross margins on software: ~82–86% adjusted (subscriptions + O&M). Professional services became a smaller % of revenue as platforms scaled. US commercial revenue exploded (+137% YoY in Q4 2025 to $507M; +109% FY2025). Overall revenue: +29% in 2024, +59% in 2025, +70% in Q4 2025. Operating expenses as % of revenue dropped sharply ( from 87% in 2022 to 69% in 2024; sales & marketing fell as % of revenue even as growth accelerated). 

People are the biggest expense (not massive fixed capex like hyperscalers). AI productivity tools + reusable Ontology mean fewer FDEs needed per dollar of revenue. They continue hiring elite talent but invest in AIP while still expanding margins.

Palantir often eats upfront implementation risk and gets paid on measurable results (Airbus 33% production boost, bp triple-digit ROI). This builds trust and sticks customers, but the platform now delivers those results faster/cheaper.

Conclusion: 

Under Dr. Alex Karp’s leadership, Palantir has engineered a fundamentally different software model, one where the platform is effectively “free” to customers upfront, years ahead of competitors, and engineered for outsized, compounding growth that few (if any) enterprise software companies can match.

Karp’s long-term, first-principles vision rooted in building for the hardest problems, maintaining a high-talent-density culture that tolerates short-term pain for decade-long payoffs, and refusing to commoditize created the Ontology and AIP as a reusable “operating system” for real-world operations. Customers get immediate, production-grade AI value through 5-day AIP Bootcamps without massive upfront commitments or risky pilots. Palantir absorbs the implementation risk, demonstrates measurable outcomes on the customer’s own data ( 33% production lifts, triple-digit ROI), and only scales revenue as value is proven

This “software is free until it prints results” approach flips traditional SaaS economics: low customer acquisition friction, near-75% bootcamp conversion, and explosive within-account expansion (139%+ NDR) with minimal additional cost. The platform’s 5–10 year technical lead governance, provenance, agentic workflows grounded in real ontology means competitors are still selling PowerPoint while Palantir deploys live digital twins.

While 🦖peers chase incremental AI features and wrestle with integration debt, Dr.Karp bet on building far ahead has produced higher growth velocity, superior margins, and stickier customer relationships. The model doesn’t just win deals it turns customers into ecosystems that expand organically for years.

I will leave you with one Dr. Karp quote: 

“The culture of this company has always been and continues to be the precondition for the creation of our software... It is that culture that allows and encourages some four thousand people to band together in shifting and often odd amalgamations to build something of value to themselves and to the world.”

Video Source: Q1 2025 Earning Call

tweet.text:
My top 6 positions currently make up ~55% of my main portfolio:

$RKLB
$IONQ
$AAPL
$PLTR
$AMZN
$GOOG

the rest of the port is diversified across a couple dozen positions all with ~1-3% allocation

Losers ultimately get cut, I let winners ride, and I add selectively on dips. Recently, I also started scraping profits more often on pumps.

Managing a portfolio of this size is practically a full time job. But I love it, it keeps me busy, and its outperformed S&P by a long shot for many years 😎

I know stock picking isn't for everyone. If I didn't have the time and patience for this strategy, I'd rely a lot heavier on indexes 😆 and I'm super happy to have my X audience to share the journey 🤝
